title: Measuring Entanglement in Qubit System
abstract: An operational way of measuring entanglement in a balanced two-path interferometers is presented, where path information is carried by some internal degree of freedom which, in turn, gets entangled with an ancilla system. The analysis is based on a tripartite description involving paths, an internal qubit degree of freedom, and some ancillary states entangled with the internal degree of freedom. It is then applied to two physically distinct experimental situations: a modified Stern-Gerlach interferometer with spin-1/2 particles and a Mach-Zehnder interferometer with photons carrying polarization. The ancilla degree of freedom may not be experimentally accessible. Tracing out the ancillary system, and employing a quantum erasing procedure based on the internal degree of freedom, it is demonstrated that a concurrence-based measure of the entanglement, between the internal degree of freedom and the ancilla, can be extracted directly from measurable asymmetry of the two output channels. These results show that loss of coherence, quantum erasure, and entanglement estimation in interferometric experiments arise from the same underlying correlation structure and provide a compact experimentally accessible framework for quantifying entanglement in qubit systems.
summary: This paper presents a novel, operational method to measure entanglement in qubit systems by analyzing interference experiments. It demonstrates that the entanglement content can be extracted directly from the measurable asymmetry observed in the output channels of both spin and photon interferometers. This unifies the understanding of decoherence, quantum erasure, and entanglement estimation into a single, compact framework.
main_problem: The paper aims to develop an operational and experimentally accessible method to quantify entanglement in qubit systems using interferometric measurements.
main_result: It establishes a unified framework showing that entanglement, coherence loss, and quantum erasure are all governed by the same underlying correlation structure, allowing concurrence to be calculated directly from measurable output channel asymmetries.
method: The analysis uses a tripartite description involving paths, an internal qubit degree of freedom, and ancillary states, deriving the entanglement measure (concurrence) from the measured asymmetry of the output channels after quantum erasure.
model_or_system: The theory is applied to two distinct physical platforms: a modified Stern-Gerlach interferometer for spin-1/2 particles and a Mach-Zehnder interferometer for polarization-carrying photons.
key_observables: The measurable asymmetry of the two output channels, which is directly related to the overlap of ancilla states ($	ext{Re}\langle\alpha| etaangle$) and determines the concurrence.
important_parameters: The overlap parameter $s = \langle\alpha| etaangle$ and the resulting concurrence $C_2 = 1 - |\langle\alpha| etaangle|^2$.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the ability to perform quantum erasure via measurement in a complementary basis and that the ancillary degree of freedom, while potentially inaccessible, dictates the measurable correlation structure.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the modified Stern-Gerlach setup using spin states, while Figure 2 details the Mach-Zehnder interferometer geometry incorporating polarization post-selection for quantum erasure.
paper_structure: The paper first introduces the tripartite model, applies it to the spin and photon interferometers, derives the relationship between the measured asymmetry and the overlap parameter, and finally establishes the universal operational entanglement law $C_2 = 1 - A^2$.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, unified theoretical tool linking fundamental concepts like entanglement, decoherence, and quantum erasure into a single, experimentally testable diagnostic based on interference asymmetries.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Modified Stern-Gerlach interferometer for using the spin states as path markers. The spin, in addition, is en- tangled with an ancilla qubit. Another Stern-Gerlach setup is employed after this stage (no shown) with field in the x- direction, to implement quantum erasure before final detec- tion.

FIG. 2. Mach–Zehnder interferometer geometry. PBS1 cre- ates two path amplitudes |ψ1⟩, |ψ2⟩, BS2 recombines them, and output polarizers enable quantum-eraser post-selection before detection at D1, D2.
